speaker: interviewer
text: This is the beginning of tape number six, interview with Mr. Harry Schlesinger. It is March the 30th, 1997. When we left off, Mr. Schlesinger, you had managed to get a job peeling potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Potatoes for the SS. It was a terrific job because if you finish the job an hour earlier you go to your barrack and don't have to work. All you had to do, there were two big barrels. You had to-- each child had to make two big barracks-- two big barrel of potatoes. And if you finished it before the time, you could go back to your barrack, what is not far from the kitchen. And you could take a rest. Now, I had big problem with my feet. And I forget if I don't-- if I go to the hospital, you can only go into the hospital. You don't go out. It's like they hear the cockroach there, that as it goes in, but you don't go out. That's how it was there, at the hospital. And I met a boy. We had a maid, Jewish maid in Europe, in Mukacevo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this boy was her boyfriend. His name was Moshe. And I meet him there. And I said, Moshe, what are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I am already here a long time. I made this 10 kilometer here and there for work. I said, Moshe, I have to-- I have a-- for you, a good deal. And if you want to help me, because otherwise I'll die. I can't survive with this pains what I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, what do you want? I say, I'm going to steal potatoes from the kitchen. You're going to cook the potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how you're going to cook it. You're going to heat up the potatoes. And I'm going to take the hot water, in my plate, what I'm eating my soup, and I'm going to soak my feet. Salt, I had there, also in the kitchen. And as I said, after two days, the best operation. I healed my feet, because hot water and salt, and this green stuff disappeared. So he saved my life. We were there approximately, in this Melk camp, I would say between 1 and 1/2 or 2 months. In fact, I met here a friend of mine, Tommy [CZECH], who was from another town next to Mukacevo, [CZECH]. And we are here, the best friends. And he-- he is reminding me each time, how was it. There was a barrack. They were mostly the young boys, between 12 and 15. And maybe the two of us was Jews. Only the rest were only Polish, from Polish parents. Those who were in politic involved. And one evening, it was a cold evening. And everybody went-- there was a little stove there to warm. And there is standing, this Polish couple. I think his name was [POLISH]. He knows exactly, because he told me that after the war they caught them and they hanged them. This was a sadist. And because he knew that I'm a Jew, and I'm also going to warm up myself-- you know, there is in every joke, there is something truth. They say, I'll give you a slap. You're going to see stars. He gave me one in my face. I literally saw stars. Then I figured myself better stay out from this stove and don't get stars. So that's what happened. We were in a barrack where three floors of beds were there. And maybe it's not ethic to tell you, but I have to tell you how it was. Sometime I had to go, middle of the night, to the toilet. I was afraid. Because if I go down, they wouldn't say that I go down to the toilet. They would tell me you came to steal from the children, bread. So the same plate which I ate the soup daytime, I had to urinate into the plate, keep it there by the bed, and in morning throw it out. Because it's still better than to get killed because I'm going to steal bread. After a month and a half, yeah, there was a very interesting place. Everybody was asking me outside I should-- if I'm in kitchen. They knew I'm in kitchen. And I'm Jewish. And the Polish guys, they trusted me, who went to the commander. Especially this Moshe, who knew already I can get potatoes. He says, bring-- bring more potatoes. I said, Moshe, it's not so easy. If they catch me, we have there a couple, a Greek guy. And this Greek guy always used to say [GREEK]. [GREEK] means, probably, no stealing. One guy, what-- a guy, they caught him. And they gave him 25 from behind. It was terrible. But still, and when they me more even than besides potatoes, they told me, bring us salt.

speaker: interviewer
text: You get for salt, everything. You get cigarette. You get bread. I said, why the hell you need so much salt? He says, when we have a portion of bread, and we put a lot of salt, we're drinking a lot of water after it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if the stomach is full, who cares with what, what as long as it's full, we are full. We are not hungry! So I figure myself, I have to do something about it to get to the-- to the salt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I made fast my job, in order to go faster. And the couple wasn't there, where the sack from the salt was. And I took my hat and pushed into the salt and got to the salt. Can you imagine, I was so rich already? I could make investment, buy cigarettes. Like, what shall I do with so much salt? I cannot put it back, the salt. So I figure, I'll buy cigarettes, put it away. When I need bread, I'll buy it. Not one day already, I have, already, a stack of cigarettes. And I go and buy-- what I buy, a piece of bread for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was a greener, you know, a greenhorn. I didn't know about the experience, how to buy this cigarette, what stocks to invest. And I didn't know how to examine the cigarette.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they did. They did-- they took from a cigarette-- they made on the paper, a cigarette from the regular-- how do you call it? From the root of whatever it is, they made like a would-be tobacco. Inside was the artificial tobacco.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the two side they was putting there the real tobacco. So when I want to smell it, I should feel this is tobacco. But there guys, they knew the trick. Before I gave him the cigarette, he broke it in two pieces and shows me, this is wood. He didn't know that I bought it like this. He thought I'm going to cheat him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He beat me up. I will never forget this beating, because actually I wanted to cheat him. So that's what happened with my business over there. Now, we were there approximately, I would say, between a month and a half and two months, in Melk. From Melk, they took us back to Mauthausen, because the American were coming closer and closer. And they wanted to evacuate the Melk camp. This time they didn't-- they didn't took us to regular Mauthausen camp, where on the hill. They took us in a valley, where made tent, like a five, six-floor building. That's how tall it was. The tent was torn. The rain was raining in it. And we were standing on the mud, no bed, nothing. And there we got-- the portion of the food was daily a slice of a bread. But when they gave you the slice of the bread, the inside was green, fall out. And we got only the outside. This was the portion of the bread. Everybody was hungry. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, people used to fight there, not to sleep, can you imagine, in the mud, to stay days, no cover, nothing? And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was only one way to get another slice of bread, if you took the bodies to another place where they burning the bodies. So I figured myself, I will do it. I wanted to pick up one of the body. But who was so strong to pick up a body? So I couldn't do this job anymore. So we were there. And from there, Mauthausen, they took us, again, walk. And they took us-- of course, it wasn't so cold anymore. And they took us to Gunskirchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gunskirchen was a camp. They say there were, there, approximately around 20,000 or 25,000 Jews. And also there was a transport who was came from Vienna, Hungarian families was the last minute. The Germans and the Hungarian chased them to this place. The men, they died the first week. They couldn't survive. The women, somehow they survived. And it was two weeks before. Because we became free in May 5th, 1945. And about two weeks before we were prepared, already, to be destroyed. Because the German get a-- got a order to destroy the whole camp. They made a grave, what we had to dig, this grave. If I would tell you a grave, what, maybe 10 times as big as this room. Had to be deep at least like, two floors deep. And it was prepared, the grave. We prepared for ourself, the grave. Soon as they get the order, the Germans, they all throwing in there. But this grave had a second purpose, not just to bury us when we are dead. There was no facilities, toilet facilities. So anybody who wanted to go to-- please forgive me for saying it. He had to stay-- if this is the opening, you had to stand next to the opening and arrange your arrangement. Whoever fall in, in this grave, because it was very big, he stayed there. He choked in human waste. This was the grave. The hunger was so big already. Yeah, before I finish this, I have to tell you, again comes the Red Cross. And everybody's getting one coffee spoon of sugar. So have to stay in the line. The line has to be straight. And we're waiting, already, for the Red Cross is giving us sugar. At the SS comes. And before he wants to give you the spoon of sugar, he looks there, straight. And the line is not straight. Piff, piff, piff. All these people around got shot right away because he was not straight in the line for the spoon of sugar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody walked away, no more sugar. Because we figure if another few days, we become free already. So why to get killed last minute? So we didn't get the spoon sugar either.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to the barrack without the sugar. And suddenly we feel a smell, when you burning a human being. It has a real ugly smell. And that's what I felt in Auschwitz also. And they caught a guy. We were there already a year, in concentration camp. We were already very skinny. So there is no meat, nothing. But those Hungarians, we were still with-- they had a little behind, they took him middle of the night. They choked him. They cut out the meat and they made a meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the German find out. And he comes into the barrack, the German. And he's asking, what is deserving, a person who is eating human beings? So everybody says to German, [GERMAN], it means to kill him. Who did it? He showed this, and he showed this, took a few guys. Piff, piff, piff, piff. He did it because he was hungry!

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't take the hunger anymore. So the last minute, they ate human beings. That's what happened in Mauthausen. Now, Friday afternoon, around five o'clock, is arriving a jeep with a white flag, four American soldiers. And they announce that we are free. The [NON-ENGLISH], who was the main guy from the camp, went over there. They want to shake hands. I was standing there. He didn't want to shake hands with him. He took away his-- rest of the American officer, whatever he was. I don't know what kind of position he had. Just putting him just on-- on his jeep. And they took him away. Then we found out that he had already, a week before, an order to destroy us and to bury in this grave. But in order to save his own skin, and he had a paper to prove it, that order was done already, a week ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he figured by not doing this, he will save his skin. What happened to him? We don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when the American army came in, it was there, I would say, between 10,000 and 15,000 people. If they would close up the camp and just three days, give us every day a cookie or just a little small thing, they could save half of the people. But everybody went and ate. There was a big magazine. And we started to eat all kind of food. And 80% of people, they ate, they fall down. I also came into the hospital. And I was sick there about two weeks. Then when I was better, I came and I saw the-- my cousin, who is my father's brother's son. But he didn't survive. He died there, in this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you were liberated, where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the American army liberated us-- and, well, another thing, what they did, the Americans, they took nurses, SS people, to take care on us in the hospital. And most of the people sickness was that they had to run out because the-- can you imagine, you have the little stomach? And suddenly you got so much food?

speaker: interviewer
text: And it started just to come out automatically. If the German didn't-- well, this German nurse had to clean them, right? So instead of to save the cleaning, all you have to do is just one thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Close his neck, five minutes, he's dead. And next day he didn't have to clean it. This was a stupid thing to do, to take an SS, he should clean us.

speaker: interviewer
text: At least they will take from the town, German nurses, or German woman, maybe they wouldn't do it. But these SS who didn't care, who only yesterday killed anyway, this Jew, why shouldn't he do this to him? That's what they did. What is your next question? Where did you go after you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were liberated, and the American, they did the best they could. They took us in the hospital. And they took us-- if they saw the Russian was on the other side, and we were on this side with trucks, they took us on the other side. They gave the best facilities, they did whatever they could to help us. They was nice to us. And we were there a short time. And this was in Gunskirchen. And then we had to walk a few kilometer. And there the American gave us over to the Russians. They gave us over to the Russians. And we were-- from [NON-ENGLISH], they took over the Russian, us. And from [NON-ENGLISH], to [NON-ENGLISH]-- they call it [CZECH] in a Czechish name-- 90 kilometer they let us walk to arrive to this town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine sick people who are skeletons, 90 kilometer? Each night we had to sleep in another German-- not house. They didn't let us in on a house, where the animals used to sleep there, they let us in. In the barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barns, to sleep there. Every night they used to scream, fire, fire. We didn't know what-- we knew what fire means. It's fire. It's burning. When the Russian soldiers came and they want to take out from the barn a horse or a cow. So they made between them an agreement. They will start to scream fire, so the whole village will come together. They wouldn't let them to take out the horse or the cow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally after walking maybe 10 days, we arrive to [NON-ENGLISH], [CZECH]. [NON-ENGLISH], we arrive. Already the Russian are also there. And there, whatever you had, a little baggage, a little [YIDDISH], a little food, everything was taken away from us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, poor. Nothing anymore. So they was putting in-- us in a place. It was, I think, a school in [NON-ENGLISH]. And every refugee got 1500 crowns. This time was the money crown.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had a right to go free. It was-- it was-- how do you call it? [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: A subway.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, not an underground subway, a regular subway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? And we showed just the number, they didn't charge us for ticket. And we ate there. there was drinking there. And I was there approximately, I would say, about two weeks. And then we found out that the Hungarians, in Budapest, they giving 1,000 [NON-ENGLISH] for every refugee. So we packed our packages. And we-- trains, top on the train, in the train, and took a few days, I go to Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And eventually, where did you end up moving to?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Budapest, I heard somebody came to me and said, Harry, what you doing here? I say, what do you mean, what I'm doing here? I just came from there-- from [NON-ENGLISH]. He says, your parents, the whole family, your mother, your father, your sister, is waiting for you home. They don't know what happened to you. Then I figured let's go home. There was also a special school. They call it [HUNGARIAN] in Budapest. And they gave us food for nothing. They gave us a bed. There joint-- I think the American joined, took care on us. And it took me a few days. And I went home. Also, on the trains, from one train to another. Took-- also take a few weeks till I got home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. how long did you stay in Mukacevo?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mukacevo, because I came-- when I was the latest who came home. My parents came home already in January, with my sister and my mother. And I stayed there, I would say, almost the end of '45. isn't it? Roughly until end of '45. Because the Russian soldiers, the Jewish officers, they told us, from here you don't have to go. From here you have to run away. And go wait until the border side open. Because once the border is closed, he says, not even a bird is going to go through this border. And that's how we went from there to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia, we stayed there a nice couple of years, till 1949, until we got papers to go Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where in Israel did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel we lived in Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm, and how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived there till 1955.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in Haifa, is-- well, is that where you were married, in Haifa?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I married here in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: In America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What made you decide to leave Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't make a living. That's the plain word. I liked the country. I was always brought up like a Zionist. I was trying, six years. I just couldn't survive there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a store with my father, together. We just couldn't make a living. That was the only reason. I tried six years. And I just couldn't make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a trade, what was not good for the country. I was a watchmaker. If I would be brick-- would be a bricklayer, I could make, maybe, a living. But like a watchmaker, I couldn't exist. Every second Jew is a watchmaker or a doctor. So I just had to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was here in America, already 10 years. And I figured-- I wrote my father, that I want to go back, and we should make-- he says, you could come if you want. I say, but it's not so easy to make a living. So that's how I stayed here. But my heart is still in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. When did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife I met here, in America. This was in 1963.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Married. I have two sons. One is in accounting business. And one is a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names are?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is-- the older one is David Schlesinger. And the younger one is William.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's been about 50 years now, a little bit more, since the war ended. How has your experience during the war affected you after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: How it affected me? That I'm still have dreams that I'm again, in concentration camp. And why I was also so stupid to let me taken, again, in a camp. How come I didn't run away after such a bad experience? And this goes on, and on, and on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your children ever ask you about your wartime experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, they ask me. Let me put it this way. The young generation will never understand this. I mean, they're trying their best. And I'll give you a little example. I came to Israel in 1949. And I talks fluently, Hebrew. And I told someone what happened in Auschwitz, what happened here, what happened. I could see, on his face, expression. If you don't live through something, you haven't got the feeling. Yeah, you-- like you read the newspaper, something you read in a book. He looked at me, all right, he felt sorry for me. But the same time, I couldn't blame him. Because when he told me what the Arabs did in Haifa, and-- or what they did in Jordan when they occupied the-- and they took the whole yeshiva and-- and they did all kind of awful things, I didn't live through either. So something what you don't live through, you don't feel it. What you read and what is a difference in what you see. So I can't blame him. This-- what hurts me today, that this patriotism, what it was when arrived to Israel, is not anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you want to give to future generations about your experiences and about your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one message. Whenever you have a chance to talk, and mostly to non-Jews, tell them what happened. And tell them that all these books what Germany already coming out, denying the whole story, that we fabricated this phony story, tell them it's not true. It happened. That's the whole idea. More, I cannot tell them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hope never again, in the future, would happen such a barbaric, nasty, dirty things, what happened in the past. This is, I think, the worst history, the worst thing get what was done to human being. And maybe if the world would look up a little bit and start to learn, from this, something. When they took 1 and 1/2 million Romanians, the Turkish people, first World War, and the whole world forgot about it. And they will do, this time something, maybe, about it. Maybe it wouldn't get to this point, that, this should happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and the Jews should never trust the Arabs. Because there's nothing to trust them. They have only one plan, to take over this little world from the map, Israel. This is the only plan was as Arafat, and the rest of their Arabs.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll end tape six here, and we'll pick this up with tape number seven.
